architecture.summary: K-Adapter is a modular framework that keeps a frozen RoBERTa backbone and trains knowledge-specific adapter modules in parallel. Each adapter consists of stacked transformer layers with projection layers and skip connections, plugged into intermediate RoBERTa layers at positions {0, 11, 23}. Adapters receive RoBERTa hidden states as input, process them independently, and their outputs are concatenated with RoBERTa's final states for downstream tasks. No information flows between adapters, enabling independent training and continual knowledge injection without catastrophic forgetting.
trained_components: factual adapter (transformer layers + projections for relation classification)
linguistic adapter (transformer layers + projections for dependency prediction)

biblio.title: K-Adapter: Infusing Knowledge into Pre-Trained Models with Adapters

claims.statement: K-Adapter (F+L) achieves 1.38% improvement over RoBERTa on OpenEntity entity typing

claims.metric: macro F1 score, micro F1 score, strict accuracy
claims.statement: K-Adapter (F+L) improves macro by 2.88%, micro by 2.54% and accuracy by 1.60% over WKLM on FIGER
K-Adapter (F+L) achieves 61.96 EM and 67.31 F1 on SearchQA, a 4.01% point improvement over WKLM
K-Adapter (F+L) achieves 72.04 F1 on TACRED relation classification, outperforming RoBERTa at 71.25 F1
Each adapter model has approximately 42M parameters, much smaller than RoBERTa's 355M, making training memory efficient
K-Adapter (F+L) significantly outperforms K-Adapter (w/o knowledge), demonstrating performance gains come from infused knowledge rather than additional parameters
K-Adapter captures richer factual knowledge than RoBERTa as evidenced by LAMA probing experiments, where K-Adapter generates more accurate object predictions for cloze-style factual questions

Factual adapter is pre-trained on relation classification using 5.5M sentences from T-REx, covering 430 relations; linguistic adapter is pre-trained on dependency relation prediction using 1M examples from Book Corpus with Stanford Parser
